quote: hoping y'all come and put it in.
stewart_read: Johnny opens with unprompted readiness — he's not waiting to be persuaded, he's expressing a desire for the install to happen. This is the strongest possible starting signal for a setter call. Parker acknowledges it briefly ('We wanna come show you the design') but does not anchor on it or use it to build momentum into the verify section.
coaching_implication: Show Parker this line and ask: 'What did you do with this?' The codex says interest_in_solar is the emotional anchor — depth matters more than the answer. Parker had a customer handing him the anchor unprompted and moved straight to address verification. In the 1-on-1, walk through what a 60-second expansion on this moment would have sounded like.

quote: yeah. I can. And, like I said, I have no problem on cutting them down either, anything in the day.
stewart_read: This is the moment that dissolves the tree-obstruction barrier. Johnny is not just saying the trees can come down — he is saying he will do it proactively, on any timeline, to make the install happen. Parker hears this and continues asking clarifying questions about the trees rather than pivoting to 'great, so the site works — let's get you on the calendar.' The barrier Parker has been treating as a blocker is explicitly removed here.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may be gathering enough site detail to brief the design team before booking. Rejected — the photo plan at 03:15 already handles that; continuing to probe the tree situation after this line is redundant.
coaching_implication: This is the pivot point Parker missed. Once Johnny says 'no problem cutting them down, anything in the day,' the site qualification question is answered. The manager should replay 02:18 and ask Parker: 'What should have happened next?' The answer is a bridge to verify and bill anchoring, not more tree questions.

quote: right now, my life, the electric company went crazy. They sent me a $2,300.
stewart_read: Johnny volunteers a $2,300 bill mid-conversation — unprompted, emotionally loaded ('the electric company went crazy'). This is the bill anchor the codex says must be captured AND flipped within 4 minutes. Parker's response is a third clarifying question about the trees ('So you said there are there some trees already cut down...'). The bill is not acknowledged, not flipped, and not returned to for the remainder of the call. The failure here is not that the bill wasn't captured as a data point — Parker heard it — but that there was no acknowledgment and no flip into emotional alignment-against-the-utility.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may be deferring the bill discussion until after the photo is received and a design can be built. Rejected — the codex is explicit that the flip is the emotional work of the setter call, not the closer's. Deferring it entirely means the customer's urgency is never activated.
coaching_implication: This is the highest-leverage missed moment in the call. In the 1-on-1, play the call to 02:29, pause, and ask Parker what he heard. Then walk through the flip: 'The electric company went crazy — $2,300 is exactly why we're calling. That's the bill solar replaces.' No fabricated numbers needed; Johnny gave Parker the number. The codex's objection_inversion pattern applies directly here.

quote: Well, right now I'm training here, but I'm thinking I'll try to get you one and
stewart_read: Johnny discloses he is currently in a training session — a real-time availability signal. Parker acknowledges it implicitly ('Okay. Gotcha.') but does not use it to anchor a specific callback time. 'I'm training right now but I'll get you a photo later today' is a natural opening to say: 'Perfect — so you'll be free around [time]? Let me lock that in as our callback.' Parker lets it pass as a logistics note rather than a scheduling anchor.
coaching_implication: Show Parker this line and ask: 'What did Johnny just tell you about his schedule?' The training disclosure is an implicit time anchor — he's busy now, free later. Parker had the information needed to lock a specific callback time and didn't use it. This is the difference between a soft callback and a booked follow-up.

quote: it's, I've got two trees that can handle the, hail. And I'm not trying to I'll take these both of them down.
stewart_read: Johnny's second explicit tree-removal commitment also contains an embedded roof/hail concern — he mentions trees that 'can handle the hail' in the context of wanting them removed. This is a natural probe point for verify.roof_type_age: how old is the roof, has it taken hail damage, is a replacement needed? Parker does not pick up the hail reference. The tree-removal commitment itself is a third signal that the site obstruction is not a disqualifier.
coaching_implication: Two things for the manager to surface here: (1) the hail mention is a verify.roof_type_age probe opportunity Parker missed — 'You mentioned hail, how's the roof holding up? How old is it?' (2) This is the third time Johnny commits to removing trees; by this point Parker should have been well past the site question and into verify/bill anchoring.

quote: I know, I talked to one guy, and they were talking about, I'm gonna have a trailer sitting in front of me.
stewart_read: Johnny references a prior conversation with 'one guy' about solar — this is a verify.prior_designs signal. The context is unclear (the trailer reference is hard to parse), but the prior contact is real. Parker does not probe it: who was the company, what did they show him, what happened? This is competitive context that would materially inform the closer's approach.
coaching_implication: Parker needed one follow-up question here: 'Oh, you talked to someone before — did they show you a design?' The answer shapes the closer's entire framing. If Johnny has a prior quote, the closer needs to know. This is a 10-second verify item Parker walked past.

quote: Yeah. Just send me a photo when you can. We'll start on our end, and then we can kinda figure out a time over text. Would that work for you?
stewart_read: Parker closes with a fully open-ended follow-up — no date, no time, no locked callback. The codex's button_up section requires appointment datetime confirmed, one-hour block stated, phone appointment specified, no-driving/computer anchor, and 'is that fair' handshake. None of these are present. 'Figure out a time over text' is the codex's soft-callback failure mode.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Parker may be intentionally leaving the time open because the photo is needed before a design can be built, and a locked appointment without a design is premature. Partially valid — but the codex's button_up protocol applies to the callback itself, not just the closer appointment. Parker could have locked a specific time to receive the photo and follow up, which would have been a booked callback rather than a loose one.
coaching_implication: Walk Parker through the button_up checklist against this call: zero of six elements present. Then ask: 'What was stopping you from saying — I'll call you back at 5pm today once you've sent the photo, does that work?' The photo logistics don't prevent a locked callback time. This is the structural gap that turns a warm lead into a no-show risk.

quote: Like I said, I'd like y'all to come on and take a look every time you want. You know?
stewart_read: Johnny's closing line is a third unprompted expression of readiness — he is inviting Ion to come out on any timeline. The energy leak in this call is entirely on Parker's side. A customer this motivated, with a $2,300 bill and explicit willingness to remove trees, should have ended this call with a locked appointment or at minimum a locked callback time. Parker responds with 'Alright. We'll talk to you a little bit later then' — no time, no anchor, no handshake.
coaching_implication: This line is the coaching closer for the 1-on-1. Play it for Parker and ask: 'What did this customer just tell you?' Then contrast it with how the call ended. The gap between Johnny's motivation and Parker's close is the entire coaching story of this call.
